people are aware, and certainly Hardiman does not mention it, that
there was an issue of the Galway Post so early as 1782. That, how-
ever, we now know to be the fact. But with all these titles it 1s a
matter much to be lamented that so few copies of any journal are ex-
tant. It is to be hoped that those, in whose hands the volumes of the
Connaught Journal from 1792 onwards are, will preserve them for the
benefit of posterity. When we get into the beginning of the nineteenth
century we find a different class of works published in Galway by George
Connolly, who appears to have been the next printer of importance
after Ilaherty and Conway, and we notice works of the school book
class and those quaing little ‘“ Song Books ’’ that have now ceased to be
printed, and also two or three chap books. At the year 1806 in this
list, I have mentioned that this was the first year of printing in Galway
known to Power and Cotton, and it is pleasant to record that thanks
to Hardiman we can carry back printing more than ﬁfty‘years earlier
than that date. Any refernce to Galway newspapers occurring in
Dublin Journals is also inserted. Under the date, 1812, we come
across a new name of a Galway printer, Ford. When I say a new
name I mean one that we have not found recorded before. Perhaps
some of the readers of this journal may be able to give fui:the_r- informa-
tion as to the works of Galway printers whose names appear in this list,
or others. We also find another printer’s name, that of Staci, under
date of 1816, where he appears as the printer of the Galway Chroni-
cle,” then in its Sth vol. I hope to continue this list on a future oc-
casion. The items are interosting and deserve to be put on record.
